title: Fixing Incorrect Type Inference for a Generic Math Calculator

statement.markdown: A simple calculator that performs basic arithmetic operations (addition, subtraction, multiplication, division) on two numbers. Modify the existing function to support generic numbers and improve its type inference.
statement.inputFormat: The input will be a string in the format 'operation number1 number2', where operation is one of 'add', 'subtract', 'multiply', 'divide'.
statement.outputFormat: The output will be the result of the operation as a string. If the operation is 'divide' and the second number is zero, return 'Error: Division by zero'.
statement.notes: The calculator should support both integer and floating-point numbers. The function should be able to handle invalid inputs and return an error message.

constraints.description: Numbers involved in the operation will be between 1 and 10000.

examples.explanation: The calculator returns an error message when dividing by zero.

hints.text: Use TypeScript's generics to support different types of numbers.

editorial.title: Improving Type Inference for a Generic Math Calculator
editorial.intuition: To improve type inference, we need to specify the type of numbers that the calculator will work with. We can use generics to support different types of numbers.
editorial.bruteForce: We can use if-else statements to check the type of operation and then apply the corresponding logic.
editorial.betterSolution: We can use a switch statement to handle different operations and use generics to support different types of numbers.
editorial.optimalSolution: Use the switch statement and generics to support different types of numbers and improve type inference.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The code is correct because it uses generics to support different types of numbers and improves type inference.

functionSignature.javascript: function calculate(operation: string, num1: number, num2: number): string
